mnemonics: Imagine Byzantine generals (computers) trying to agree on a plan (consensus) despite traitors (faulty nodes). The acronym BFT (Byzantine Fault Tolerance) stands for 'By-Failproof-Togetherness'.
keyPoints: The Byzantine Generals Problem illustrates the challenge of achieving consensus in a distributed system with potentially malicious or faulty components.
It requires a reliable system to function correctly even if some nodes fail or act maliciously.
Blockchain solves this by using consensus algorithms (like Proof of Work or Proof of Stake) to achieve Byzantine Fault Tolerance.
commonMistakes: Assuming the problem is only about malicious actors; it also encompasses simple system failures and faults.
Believing a simple majority (51%) is always sufficient; some Byzantine faults require a higher threshold (e.g., 2/3 consensus).
